topic: MySpace profile culture and the tyranny of the Top 8
hook: Social ranking became a mainstream teen ritual, and the platform’s design turned friendship drama into user interface.
notes: Strong built-in humor, conflict, and recognizable cultural texture.

topic: AIM away messages as performance art
hook: Teenagers used status text like diary entries, flirtation, passive aggression, and tiny literary salons.
notes: Great for voice and internet-behavior analysis; likely needs archived contemporaneous writing.

topic: LiveJournal oversharing, fandom, and locked-post politics
hook: Blogging, fandom, intimacy, and social fragmentation all collided in one exquisitely dramatic platform.
notes: Rich but could sprawl; needs a disciplined angle.

topic: Perez Hilton and the blog-era celebrity snark economy
hook: Messy, mean, wildly influential, and central to a whole mode of millennial media consumption.
notes: Strong receipts likely available; tone needs sharpness without becoming lazy cruelty.

topic: The rise and fall of Juicy Couture tracksuit civilization
hook: Aesthetic excess, celebrity culture, aspirational mall-girl economics, and national delusion in velour.
notes: Probably strong for a future post if tied to status performance and tabloid-era femininity.

topic: Yahoo Answers as the internet’s great hall of sincere questions and magnificent nonsense
hook: A platform built for information retrieval became a public stage for oversharing, accidental comedy, and crowdsourced absurdity.
notes: Clear hook, strong nostalgia factor, and plenty of contemporaneous coverage plus memorable artifacts.

topic: Quizilla and the personality-quiz industrial complex
hook: Before algorithmic feeds knew who you were, teenagers volunteered the data in endless quizzes about crushes, aesthetics, and secret selves.
category: identity play / teen internet / participatory culture
notes: Distinct from MySpace and LiveJournal: focuses on quizzes as identity performance and viral self-sorting.

topic: Neopets guild economies and virtual pet capitalism
hook: Children built financial systems, social hierarchies, and black markets inside a browser game about feeding cartoon animals.
category: virtual economies / gaming culture / kid internet
notes: Strong intersection of economics, community governance, and early internet childhood.

topic: GeoCities neighborhood culture and the hand-coded homepage era
hook: Before social media profiles, people built entire websites by hand and sorted themselves into themed neighborhoods like digital suburbanites.
category: web culture / DIY internet / platform history
notes: Foundational internet culture artifact; Yahoo's deletion of GeoCities is itself a major story.

topic: Web rings and internet discovery before Google
hook: Finding websites used to be a social act — you followed hand-curated loops of links because no algorithm existed to do it for you.
notes: Pairs well with GeoCities; illustrates how discovery worked before search dominance.

topic: Napster, Limewire, and the moral panic of music piracy
hook: A generation learned to steal music and felt righteous about it, and the recording industry responded by suing teenagers.
category: music / piracy / digital rights / moral panic
notes: Massive cultural footprint; strong tension between industry, technology, and teen behavior.

topic: SmarterChild and the proto-chatbot era on AIM
hook: Before Siri or ChatGPT, millions of bored teenagers talked to a bot on AIM that could check weather, do math, and absorb casual cruelty.
notes: Interesting proto-AI angle; connects early internet culture to current moment.

topic: Xanga and the teenage blog-diary boom
hook: A platform purpose-built for teenage oversharing became a mass confessional booth where melodrama was the default register.
notes: Distinct from LiveJournal in audience and tone; more mainstream teen, less fandom-driven.

topic: DeviantArt and the online art community hierarchy
hook: Amateur artists built a sprawling ecosystem of favorites, critiques, and social capital that trained a generation before Instagram existed.
category: art communities / platform culture / creative internet
notes: Rich community dynamics; later controversies around AI art training add contemporary resonance.

topic: Homestar Runner and the Flash animation golden age
hook: A cast of weird characters in a hand-animated web series became the shared reference point for an entire generation of internet kids.
notes: Emblematic of pre-YouTube internet entertainment; Strong Bad emails are a cultural touchstone.

topic: Away message emo lyrics and the passive-aggressive subtweet before Twitter
hook: Teenagers weaponized song lyrics in AIM away messages to send coded signals about crushes, fights, and existential despair.
category: messaging culture / music / teen social dynamics
notes: Narrower slice of AIM culture than vce-002; focused specifically on lyric-as-communication and proto-subtweeting.

topic: The Sims and the suburban fantasy sandbox
hook: A game about managing fake lives became a canvas for projecting desires, testing cruelty, and rehearsing adulthood in miniature.
notes: Massive cultural footprint; intersects gender, class fantasy, and creative play.

topic: Chain emails, surveys, and the forward-this-to-10-people social contract
hook: Inbox culture turned friendship into a compliance test: forward this, fill this out, prove you love your friends, and maybe avoid supernatural bad luck.
category: email culture / viral rituals / social pressure
notes: Distinct from AIM and blogging: focuses on email-era virality, superstition, and friendship performance before algorithmic sharing.

topic: FanFiction.Net disclaimers, flame wars, and the Mary Sue tribunal
hook: Young internet writers built a sprawling amateur literature scene policed by disclaimers, review culture, and brutal community norms about cringe, taste, and originality.
notes: Adjacent to LiveJournal fandom but materially different: centered on amateur writing platforms, review drama, and anti-Mary-Sue gatekeeping.

topic: Facebook pokes, wall posts, and the etiquette of low-stakes social contact
hook: Early Facebook ran on tiny ambiguous gestures — a poke, a wall post, a relationship-status change — that let college-age users perform interest, status, and surveillance without saying much at all.
category: social internet / platform behavior / college culture
notes: Distinct from MySpace: cleaner interface, real-name culture, and a different set of social rules built around understatement rather than maximal self-expression.

topic: Early YouTube before the algorithm: reply videos, five-star ratings, and chaotic amateur celebrity
hook: Before creators chased watch time, YouTube felt like a weird public-access experiment where reply girls, awkward vloggers, and accidental stars invented online video culture in real time.
category: video culture / platform behavior / amateur media
notes: Distinct from Flash-era animation: this is about early YouTube’s social mechanics, low-fi fame, and pre-professional creator culture.

topic: LOLcats, Failblog, and the meme-network empire of I Can Has Cheezburger?
hook: A deliberately stupid cat caption style turned into a full publishing machine, proving that internet in-jokes could become scalable media businesses.
notes: Clear tension between disposable joke culture and actual media monetization; distinct from Yahoo Answers because it centers image-meme publishing rather than user Q&A absurdity.
